questions.q: Hermes Agent is best described as a:
questions.explain: Hermes wraps a model and gives it the ability to read files, run commands, and persist across sessions. That's an agent harness.

questions.q: Name one thing Hermes can do that a regular chatbot cannot.
questions.explain: Any of: read and write files; run shell commands; remember context across sessions; schedule tasks; connect to Discord/Linear/Notion via MCP.

questions.q: You want to build a full-stack web app from scratch. Which kind of harness first?
choices: Neither — you need a no-code tool
questions.explain: Coding harnesses verify by running tests, which is what you need for shipping software.

questions.q: True or false: Hermes is an AI model. Explain in one sentence.
questions.explain: False. Hermes is a harness, not a model. You bring your own model (Claude, GPT, DeepSeek, Kimi) and Hermes wraps it.

questions.q: Per the source video, what is the trade-off when using an agent harness instead of a coding harness?
choices: Agent harnesses are slower at every task.
Agent harnesses verify by inferring, not by running code.
Agent harnesses can't use frontier models.
There is no trade-off; agent harnesses are strictly better.
questions.explain: Agent harnesses verify by inferring ("does this look right?") rather than running tests. More flexible for ongoing work, less reliable for code.

questions.q: What does BYOK stand for, and why does it matter for Hermes?
questions.explain: "Bring Your Own Key." Hermes doesn't bundle model access — you sign up with a provider (Anthropic, OpenAI, etc.) and paste your API key. Cost is whatever you spend on the model.
